region_of_origin: Italy (origin); globally commercialised as packaged beverage

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to packaged or ready-to-drink cappuccino exists; A0FKP (coffee-based beverages) is used as the closest parent category. The packaged/RTD cappuccino format is a commercial sub-type not individually enumerated in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged cappuccino beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 240 ml (one can or bottle), equivalent to roughly 240 g, providing about 103 kcal and 20 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Cappuccino (packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged cappuccino (cr_id D09010020). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest analogous RTD cappuccino product and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged cappuccino in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 53 is estimated from sweetened milk-coffee beverages (café latte with sugar, GI ~53) as the closest analogous food in the Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). A GI of 53 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (GI ≤ 55 = low per the University of Sydney classification). gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 53 × 20.2 / 100 ≈ 10.1 (based on 240 g serving × 8.4 g carbohydrate/100 g = 20.2 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.64 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 32 mg/100 g but does not offset the SSB sugar concern.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this packaged cappuccino due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification (7.64 g sugar/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold); switch to unsweetened black coffee or freshly prepared espresso-based drinks without added sugar, as added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.64 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes and is classified as unsuitable for type 2 diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this packaged cappuccino entirely, as its 7.64 g/100 g added sugar with zero fibre constitutes a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid blood glucose elevation; replace with unsweetened instant coffee or freshly brewed espresso without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk; non-dairy creamer component may contribute saturated fat from palm oil.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this packaged cappuccino due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification and potential saturated fat contribution from palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer; choose unsweetened coffee alternatives and verify ingredient labels for hydrogenated or palm-derived fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and likely palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer provide pro-inflammatory inputs with no anti-inflammatory nutritional benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this packaged cappuccino as its added sugar and probable palm-oil creamer contribute pro-inflammatory dietary load; replace with unsweetened black coffee or freshly prepared cappuccino to access espresso's chlorogenic acid anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged cappuccino is a ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened coffee beverage commercially formulated to replicate the flavour profile of a traditional Italian cappuccino. It is typically made with water, milk solids or non-dairy creamer (often palm-oil-based), instant coffee or espresso extract, and added sugar. The beverage is sold in cans, bottles, or tetra-pack cartons and requires no preparation. Per 100 g it provides 43 kcal, 1.2 g protein, 0.4 g fat, 8.4 g carbohydrate, and 7.64 g sugar, with 32 mg sodium and zero dietary fibre. The taste is sweet, milky, and lightly coffee-flavoured with a smooth, thin texture. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its sugar content exceeding 5 g/100 g in liquid form, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The absence of dietary fibre means sugar is absorbed rapidly, producing a significant postprandial glycaemic response. The estimated glycaemic index is 53 (low by University of Sydney classification, GI ≤ 55), based on analogy with sweetened milk-coffee beverages. Packaged cappuccino is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a convenient breakfast or snack beverage. It is an ultra-processed product and should be consumed only occasionally by the general population.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a packaged cappuccino?
Is RTD cappuccino suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of instant cappuccino?
Is canned coffee a sugar-sweetened beverage?
Is packaged cappuccino high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010020) at confidence 0.95; this is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (sweetened milk-coffee beverage proxy) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing ingredients. 2. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the approved source, not missing data. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin/mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for this packaged cappuccino are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.3 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for canned/RTD cappuccino (closest analogous product); HPB source does not separately report saturated fat. 5. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from sweetened milk-coffee beverage data in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008); no direct measurement for packaged cappuccino exists in the approved GI database. Per the University of Sydney classification, GI ≤ 55 = low; therefore gi_category is set to 'low' for GI 53. 7. Halal status assumed based on typical commercial formulation; kosher status not confirmed. 8. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input are retained as contextually accurate (32 mg sodium/100 g; 43 kcal/100 g) but do not override the SSB classification for health suitability purposes. 9. foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'broader') to conform to the allowed enumeration: closest_parent, exact, none.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 fix 1: foodex2_match_type changed from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values (closest_parent, exact, none) as required by g5_ontology_honesty. Gap note updated accordingly.

Iteration 3 fix 2: gi_category changed from 'medium' to 'low' to correctly reflect that GI 53 falls within the low category per University of Sydney classification (GI ≤ 55 = low, GI 56–69 = medium, GI ≥ 70 = high). gi_source.source_match_notes and llm_training.natural_language_description updated to reflect the corrected category. This resolves the q4_glycemic_completeness failure from iteration 2.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
